Rich Text:
Following the final weeks of excitement, hourly calls and frenzy to launch, its eerily quiet, hopefully at least a monthly review to talk about ‘something’ (often SEO data / rankings….).

Websites are launched, ahead of this some are checked with some technical, diagnostic tools. The website is then handed over to the client, sometimes the agency has a retainer – a level of oversight, but this is limited due to available skills, client funding and disproportionality expensive diagnostic and free tools the agency my have.

Alternatively, client takes over the website, perhaps with the introduction of a ‘tool’ that offers reactive automated testing, diagnostics (SiteImprove, Sitemorse, Monsido etc).

…..what can be worse, is if is the agency that makes the introduction to the tool… WHY? The agency introduces ‘a partner’ to deal with their client, to tell their client about website issues and often compete to deliver the correction work.

These tools, are in most cases at the heart of the issue – pages of technical detail. Running every week on everything often highlighting key issues, their sporadic usage resulting in today's websites undermining value and creating unnecessary risk.

Name: Banking SEO Value
Snippet: SEMrush – getting the fundamentals right can improve organic traffic by 23% and improves keyword ranking by 27%. Using AiSC to deliver measurable visibility and client confidence.
Rich Text:
Recent research by SEMrush highlights a clear truth: getting the basics right matters. The study analysed thousands of websites and found a direct link between accessibility quality and SEO performance. Pages that improved structure, contrast, form labels, and alt text achieved higher search rankings, stronger engagement, and faster loading speeds. These are not new insights. The evidence now clearly shows how fundamental accessibility is to visibility and user experience.

Sitemorse has long supported this conclusion. Their data confirms that reducing accessibility issues improves search performance. This is not coincidence. It is how good digital practice works. Strong fundamentals such as clarity, structure, and accessibility have always supported both visibility and experience. The SEMrush study provides measurable proof that poor accessibility damages SEO performance, while fixing it improves value.

This article explores how automation and intelligence are changing the way agencies approach SEO. AiSC enables agencies and clients to identify, prioritise, and act on these fundamentals instantly. It highlights where value can be recovered, what impacts visibility, and how small actions can deliver measurable gains in a single day.

Accessibility and SEO Connection

Accessibility and SEO are often treated as separate disciplines, yet both depend on clear structure and strong digital foundations. Search engines prioritise content that is easy to interpret and navigate, while accessible websites naturally deliver that clarity. When heading levels, links, and image descriptions are correctly implemented, users and search engines can better understand relevance and context.

SEMrush found that improving accessibility metrics leads to stronger search visibility. Websites with clearer hierarchy and accurate metadata perform better in organic rankings. Accessibility is not only about compliance. It is about communication and discoverability. When information is structured correctly, it becomes usable, findable, and valuable.

AiSC builds on this principle. It continuously measures accessibility and structural quality as part of SEO fundamentals. With AiSC you can measure what matters and act on what makes a difference. By identifying where accessibility or structure limits visibility, AiSC provides actionable insight that delivers results fast.

From Accessibility to Fundamentals

Accessibility is one part of a larger picture. SEO and GEO performance rely on solid digital foundations. Up to 75% of what determines visibility depends on getting these basics right. Structure, clarity, consistency, and content delivery are the building blocks of effective optimisation. When these are correct, every marketing action becomes more efficient and cost effective.

AiSC analyses these fundamentals at scale. It ensures websites operate efficiently and that value is not lost through neglect of small issues. Traditional SEO approaches often overlook this step, focusing on keywords, content volume, or backlinks. Without the correct foundations, those efforts cannot reach their full potential. AiSC exposes these easy wins so agencies can act in hours instead of weeks.

Traditional ‘technical’ SEO vs AiSC Fundamentals Approach

  • Traditional SEO Methods
  • AiSC Fundamentals Approach
  • Manual diagnostics limited to keywords and backlinks.
  • Automated analysis across all fundamentals including accessibility, structure, and delivery.
  • Dependence on multiple experts and isolated tools.
  • A single source of clarity that identifies priorities immediately.
  • Reactive reporting and delayed action.
  • Continuous oversight highlighting value to recover quickly.
  • Focus on metrics rather than meaning.
  • Focus on what directly impacts visibility and user experience.
  • Traditional SEO remains valuable, but its manual nature limits speed and consistency. AiSC applies automation to uncover what matters most, replacing fragmented workflows with clear, evidence-based action.

AI and the GEO Shift

Search is changing. AI systems interpret and summarise information, providing answers instead of lists. This new environment, called GEO or Generative Experience Optimisation, depends on accessible, structured, and trustworthy content. Websites built on strong fundamentals are more likely to appear in AI-generated results.

The eskimoz paper “The Future of SEO in the Gen AI Era” explains how GEO builds on SEO principles. Search engines and AI models prioritise pages that are structured, authoritative, and relevant. AiSC ensures those qualities are present, making agencies and clients ready for the GEO landscape today.

AI models depend on clear, accurate data. They cannot use content that is difficult to interpret. By confirming accessibility, structure, and delivery, AiSC ensures that websites are correctly understood by both users and AI systems.

AiSC and AOD in Action

AiSC is the intelligence platform that analyses millions of pages and identifies what affects visibility and performance. The Agency Opportunity Dashboard, or AOD, is the application that presents this information in a clear and usable way. Together, they help agencies and clients understand exactly where improvements can be made and how value can be recovered.

This is progress through technology. AiSC replaces manual investigation and multi-specialist dependency with automation built on 25 years of experience and 3.7 trillion diagnostic data points. It transforms complexity into clarity, providing understanding of what truly matters.

For agencies, this means faster delivery, measurable ROI, and stronger client relationships. For clients, it means improved SEO performance, lower costs, and lasting confidence that fundamentals are under control.

Value You Can Bank

AiSC and AOD turn insight into action. They identify where small, specific changes can deliver measurable gains in visibility and engagement. For agencies, this creates new revenue opportunities supported by evidence. For clients, it provides better performance and efficiency while reducing unnecessary spend.

Automation handles complexity so teams can focus on strategy and delivery. This combination of intelligence and clarity produces faster reporting, visible progress, and stronger trust. It keeps agencies ahead of competitors and clients confident in the results.

If not already using AiSC, try www.AAAnow.ai/confirm and understand where your content stands today. Measure what matters, act on what makes a difference, and build lasting competitive advantage.

Who Uses the Agency Opportunity Dashboard

Evidence of where to act first.

Every agency depends on confidence. Confidence that client websites are performing, protected, and creating value. Each role inside an agency experiences that confidence differently. For a CEO it means margin and reputation. For a business development lead it means a reliable pipeline. For an account director it means retention. For a CFO it means predictability and control.

Each role manages a part of the same picture, but few share the same view.

The Agency Opportunity Dashboard (AOD) brings those views together. It turns continuous auditing into structured visibility that shows where value is being lost and where unnecessary risk is being created. AOD provides a single, evidence-based view of opportunity and exposure. It connects commercial outcomes to digital fundamentals and supports every role in achieving growth.

How reducing client risk and removing barriers that undermine value creates immediate revenue and confidence for your client.
For agencies, the new way to evidence opportunity.

The Sparktoro 2024 State of Digital Agencies report highlights the same priorities that AOD addresses directly: revenue growth, client relationships, and profitability. AOD helps agencies deliver all three by proving value, maintaining confidence through verified oversight, and identifying opportunities for improvement without additional complexity.

The Roles That Use AOD

Every part of an agency contributes to confidence and revenue. AOD ensures they all work from the same evidence.

Persona

Primary Goal

How AOD Helps

Auditing across clients reveals where confidence may slip and where recoverable value exists. Supports clear planning and measurable progress.

Uses evidence from recent audits to re-engage dormant accounts and prioritise prospects. Conversations begin with proof, not speculation.

Client Services Director or Account Director

Brings audit-based insight to client reviews. Turns oversight into actions that strengthen relationships.

Head of Strategy or Performance

Aligning work to outcomes

Links audited fundamentals to commercial goals. Directs effort where change creates measurable impact.

CFO or Operations Director

Predictable performance and efficiency

Converts audit visibility into financial foresight. Supports recurring revenue models built on verified progress.

CMO or Head of Marketing

Brand confidence and trust

Uses ongoing audits to demonstrate control across client estates. Shows leadership through verified governance and reduced risk.

Shared Pressures, Shared Evidence

The Alliance of Independent Agencies 2024–25 Performance Pulse shows how agencies are balancing challenge and opportunity. 38% list new business generation as their biggest challenge. 29% identify growth within existing clients as their main opportunity. 65% report rising costs that continue to affect margins.

Agencies that perform best act on what they already know. AOD turns auditing into structured visibility that can be shared across teams. It ensures that growth and retention decisions come from the same facts, not from disconnected reports or assumptions.

This shared visibility forms the foundation of productised client retention and revenue growth. It provides a repeatable, evidence-based system for building confidence and improving performance.

The CEO or Founder

For agency leaders, AOD provides clarity. It shows where the business is performing strongly and where exposure could undermine reputation or profit. Research from The Wow Company found that agencies which measure performance consistently achieve an average of 28% operating profit, compared with 18% for those that do not. AOD supports that level of discipline. It connects leadership decisions to verified data and enables more confident planning. Early visibility of declining value or growing risk helps leaders act before issues reach clients or affect results.

The Head of Business Development

For business development teams, evidence creates opportunity. The Performance Pulse notes that top-performing agencies focus on propositions supported by data-led insight. AOD provides that proof. Auditing across current and reference clients highlights recurring issues that weaken value, such as poor accessibility, outdated structures, or content duplication that harms SEO. These findings form the basis for targeted outreach. BD teams use verified examples to reconnect with prospects or revive dormant clients. This approach replaces opinion with evidence, shortens sales cycles, and converts oversight into billable opportunity.

Client Services and Account Directors

Client retention depends on trust. Clients stay with agencies that show control and continuous progress. The Wow Company found that 83% of agencies want more clients on retainers because predictable income supports better planning. AOD provides the structure that sustains those relationships. Each audit becomes a shared record of improvement and a guide for what comes next. Account reviews move from opinion to evidence. Clients see that fundamentals are being managed. Agencies maintain value and protect margin.

Head of Strategy or Performance

Strategists must decide what matters most. AOD turns diagnostic complexity into focused visibility. It connects the six AiSC fundamentals of Experience, SEO, Integrity, Accessibility, Privacy, and Carbon to measurable results such as visibility, trust, and efficiency. This connection allows strategists to align delivery with commercial outcomes. They can demonstrate how improved fundamentals raise performance, protect reputation, and reduce cost. AOD makes strategic planning practical by showing which actions create measurable impact.

CFO or Operations Director

Profitability depends on foresight. The Wow Company notes that successful agencies measure profitability by client and by service rather than only at company level. AOD builds on that approach by showing how audited fundamentals affect financial results. It highlights where unnecessary risk increases cost and where stronger fundamentals reduce workload. For operations teams, this insight improves forecasting and resource planning. Time and budget can be allocated to the accounts that deliver measurable return. Recurring revenue becomes predictable because it is based on verified progress rather than assumption.

CMO or Head of Marketing

Reputation and trust define agency brands. The Performance Pulse shows that 87% of high-performing agencies align delivery with measurable results. AOD gives marketing leaders the visibility to demonstrate this discipline. Continuous auditing shows that client sites remain accessible, compliant, and sustainable. This evidence protects the agency’s brand, strengthens reputation, and builds confidence with clients. Marketing teams can show that oversight of Accessibility, Privacy, and Carbon is part of their agency’s culture, not a reactive task.

Why Visibility Matters

Agencies that measure and act on evidence make more profit and retain more clients. The Wow Company summarises it simply: 'The more you measure, the more profit you make.' The Performance Pulse reinforces the same principle, noting that high-performing agencies combine accountability with data-led decision making. AOD delivers that alignment. Continuous auditing becomes structured visibility that every role can understand and use. It connects separate activities and turns information into actions that sustain confidence and growth.

The Shared Outcome

When all roles share the same evidence, three outcomes follow:
1. Alignment between leadership, delivery, and sales.
2. Confidence for clients built on visible oversight.
3. Predictable revenue supported by managed risk and verified progress.

Evidence shared across roles. Priorities made clear. Immediate revenue and lasting confidence.

Name: Fundamental Background
Snippet: Every organisation is now a digital organisation.
Rich Text:
Every organisation is now a digital organisation.

The website is no longer just a front door. It is the business. It drives engagement, sales, reputation, recruitment, compliance, and investor confidence.

Yet in many organisations, there is no clear visibility of how well their online presence is really performing.


Executives are often given reports that reassure more than they reveal.

These focus on surface-level wins or checkbox compliance rather than operational reality. This is not about blame. It is a consequence of complexity, fragmented systems, and a reporting culture that avoids highlighting underlying issues.

As a result, problems remain hidden. Value is lost, risk accumulates, and confidence weakens without anyone realising.

“For the first time (we believe), automation is moving beyond technical diagnostics to reveal what truly matters for performance, trust, and risk.”

AI at scale can now pinpoint the website elements that damage user experience, weaken trust, and cut into value.


As well as identifying what’s costing you money, we’re uncovering content risks that leave organisations unnecessarily exposed - and are silently eroding brand equity.

‍

‍

‍

From Tests to Clarity: How Fundamentals Were Built?

This does not mean the other ~ 1,650 checks are ignored. They have been grouped and prioritised intelligently based on real-world outcomes - not technical completeness.

The aim is not to create a long list of technical faults, but to confirm what genuinely affects value and risk.

We have always focused on substance, not standards for their own sake. What matters is not whether a site passes every test, but whether it performs, builds trust, and avoids unnecessary exposure.

‍

Our work spans; infrastructure, UX, compliance, carbon impact, and content integrity. But just running tests is not the goal. The goal is knowing which one's matter - and why. That is what we’ve spent the last two decades refining.

First published in 2004, our independent benchmarking framework has been used by public bodies, regulators, insurers, analysts, and major organisations to understand how their websites are performing and where the risks lie.

‍

Why are we the right organisation to define and deliver the measurement of digital fundamentals?

The answer is rooted in scale, depth, and trust.

With over 25 years of direct experience assessing digital performance, we have built one of the most comprehensive data sets in the sector - over 3.7 trillion data points covering every element of digital behaviour, quality, and risk.

Our technology has been used to run over 1,700 different tests across millions of pages. This has allowed us to observe patterns, compare outcomes, and understand the practical impact of each issue in context. From that foundation, we’ve been able to identify and define the core digital fundamentals - a focused group of around 20 critical  / 30 priority indicators that every web page should confirm are in place.

‍

”We have been measuring websites longer than almost anyone else”

‍

‍

Digital fundamentals are the essential building blocks that underpin a healthy online presence.

They are not creative choices or tactical features. They are structural elements that determine whether your digital operations are delivering value, creating risk, or quietly underperforming. Just as organisations rely on financial fundamentals to understand business health, digital fundamentals offer the same for online operations. Without them, everything built on top, from content to campaigns - is less effective, less trustworthy, and more exposed.

‍

“No more decoding tech speak. Just results you understand”

‍

‍

This matters at the executive level

Executives are not expected to know how websites work at a technical level.

But just as they rely on financial reporting, risk registers, or operational dashboards, they need a way to monitor digital health and performance.

Without this, three issues persist:.

  • Reputational damage caused by inaccessibility or outdated content
  • Lost revenue due to broken journeys, poor visibility, or missed compliance
  • Risk exposure through privacy violations, hidden vulnerabilities, or poor governance

These are not isolated problems.

They are well-documented and recurring across sectors. And they can be traced back to one core gap: no clear way to confirm whether the digital fundamentals are in place.

‍

Name: Legislative exposure – yours!
Snippet: 3% of agency executives understand new accessibility laws already impacting clients in key US states. As the UK and EU align, vendor liability becomes automatic.
Rich Text:
3% of Agency Executives Are Aware of What Comes Next

How changing accessibility laws are creating automatic liability for agencies and why the window for action is closing.

3% Awareness, 97% Exposure

3% of agency executives understand how fast accessibility legislation is changing. Most still believe that accessibility responsibility sits with the client, or that carefully worded terms and conditions protect them from risk. Both assumptions are now wrong.

Across new and updated regulations, a single idea appears again and again: vendor accountability. Lawmakers have recognised that digital suppliers, not clients, are often the ones making or overstating compliance claims. To address this, the latest laws now include clauses that remove the ability for vendors to hide behind their own terms and conditions. The intent is clear: responsibility cannot be contracted away.

Agencies, CMS providers, and digital suppliers are becoming ever more accountable, and with new laws legally accountable when clients’ websites or online products fail accessibility requirements. Accessibility has shifted from a design preference to a regulated standard, and that change is already written into law across several key US states. The same model is spreading internationally, from the EEA to Asia-Pacific.

Where the Law Now Sits

Accessibility requirements have been part of US procurement policy for decades, but the latest state-level updates explicitly extend responsibility to service providers.

Region / Law Focus Liability Shift Source
California AB 1757 Requires WCAG 2.1 AA conformance for websites; presumption of non-compliance when failed. Vendors directly liable for inaccessible sites. AB 1757 Text / Senate Analysis / DWT Briefing
Virginia HB 2541 Defines digital accessibility within ICT procurement. Suppliers and agencies both accountable. LIS Text / BarrierBreak / Allyant
New York Rulings require accessibility for state and municipal suppliers. Vendors responsible for digital discrimination. NY State Accessibility
Colorado Accessibility Law Applies to all state/local suppliers delivering digital services. Vendors accountable under procurement rules. Colorado Law HB21-1110
European Accessibility Act Harmonises accessibility obligations across the EU. Shared liability between operators and service providers. EEA Overview
United Kingdom Expected to align with EEA within next reform cycle. Supplier accountability likely to mirror EU. Gov UK Accessibility
South Korea / Singapore / Australia Accessibility tied to procurement standards. Vendors must meet WCAG to qualify. Microassist Blog

Why Agencies Are Now in the Firing Line

These laws were created because agencies and technology partners have repeatedly over-promised compliance. Clients relied on those claims and were left exposed. The law now treats the supplier as a party to accessibility failure unless that supplier explicitly states that compliance is not included. Even then, such disclaimers offer little protection as contract language rarely overrides civil-rights legislation.

Retrospective Risk

While current rules apply to new contracts, legislators are debating how far back accountability can reach. Some discussions propose retrospective enforcement covering prior projects by one, three, or even five years. Class actions are already being discussed where clients share common vendor claims.

The Global Echo

The same legislative structure is emerging globally. The European Accessibility Act sets shared liability, the UK is expected to follow, and similar policies are already embedded in Asia-Pacific procurement frameworks. Privacy enforcement is following a similar path, extending accountability to vendors who manage user data.

The Legal Detail Agencies Overlook

Terms and conditions that once felt watertight now offer little protection. Accessibility law prioritises user rights. If an agency's work prevents equal access, courts side with the user, not the supplier's contract. The safest route is transparency: make clear whether compliance is provided and prove it where claimed.

Risk, Exposure, and Evidence

Accessibility is the first area where supplier liability is tested, but privacy and data control will follow. Risk now sits in proposals, RFPs, and statements of compliance. Agencies must maintain audit trails and evidence to defend their position.

What Agencies Should Do Now

1. Audit your exposure and review all contracts mentioning compliance.
2. Validate every claim of accessibility.
3. Maintain documentation and testing records.
4. Align proposal language with deliverable proof.
5. Establish continuous oversight and evidence reporting.

How AOD Supports Awareness and Oversight

The Agency Opportunity Dashboard (AOD) provides clear visibility of accessibility exposure across client estates. It identifies where compliance has been promised but not verified, and tracks progress over time. AOD acts as both auditing evidence and ongoing oversight, giving agencies the certainty they need.

The Reality

Liability is written into law. Ignorance is not a defence. Only 3 % of agencies understand this change, leaving 97 % at risk. Agencies that act now can prepare. Those that wait may face difficult questions about why they did not.
